Make sure they are well ventilated or have some sort of active cooling that doesnt just burn thru your extra power generated worh this method. replies.author: @RWBHere
replies.text: The voltage per junction is fixed by the semiconductor dopants in the cells. This is usually around 0.55V per junction, on load. So once full voltage is reached, it stops rising at that point, but more current will be generated at higher illumination levels.

replies.author: @DIYwithBatteries

author: @dphillips8311

With a larger panel array, an irrigation system spraying water on the panels (recirculated via pump & guttering), yields better power, even with the pumps electrical  overhead.
Might help tackle the heating issue with lenses.

author: @Patriarchtech
text: This is awesome. I looked into this a lot. Not so much with reflections and Fresnel lenses. I found that the companies that produce the cells are actually not testing the output of their panels in broad daylight. They obviously(if you think about it) do it in a lab using sodium based light sources(Which are very expensive for Joe six pack in their configuration. Sodium based lights normally found in street lights produce a kind of "orangy" light) However I read in some of the comments in this thread, what light range PV panels work most efficiently at,  and visible was assumed. However this is not true, if you place a powerful grow light in front of your pv panel it can in fact produce what is similar to a manufacturing testing environment or at least close to it. Now if you have a look at grow lights there has been a lot of development in these over the last decade, especially with regards to LEDs. LED growlight typically will have have a combination of blue and red LEDs and they can be bought in LED strip form relatively cheap. The visible range is as you stated Robert also important, but i recon a an optimal combination of normal LEDs (6400k W and above) and grow LEDs in terms of watt usage is interesting in this equation. Because if you can get a combination with reflectors and lights it might be beneficial to set up some lights in front of your panels and have them work at night. Light from the sun can be replicated......Fresnel and reflectors make this even more exciting.

replies.author: @stuhacker9878

author: @TheAprone
text:
I've often seen people building their own rotating platforms so the solar panel follows the sun.  I've seen people line up multiple of these rigs to scale up the number of panels.  Is there any reason why you can't stick to the smaller number of static panels, but have rotating mirrors to bounce the sun onto them?  It seems you could scale up the cheap mirrors and, actually pretty cheap, tracking rigs to get more power out of a small number of expensive solar panels.
I'm guessing there is some obvious reason why this is a bad idea, because I've seen maybe a hundred diy setups to move the solar panel but not yet anyone moving mirrors.

author: @boots7859
text:
Difficult to hear, however it sounds like your saying solar panel only is .768A, and 802.799 ?
Or .768 to .802?  Doesn't seem like 10-20%.  Take some white poster board, cut out the area in the center where the solar panel will site and test that. IIRC, this was reported to increase SP output close to 10%. 
One thing you should do is check for how much increase you get when the panel is not directly oppsosing the sun as you have in the test setup to mimic normal sun movement in the sky as the day goes on.
I would expect gains to drop a fair bit.  Averaged across the day, the gains are likely quite a bit lower than original test results.

replies.author: @TimeSurfer206
replies.text:
You're failing to factor in that, as the angle of incidence on the panels increases, the light bouncing onto it from the mirrors is making up for a lot of loss.
This allows us to get some of the benefits of a tracking system without having to pay for a tracking system.

author: @boots7859
text: Haven't watched the video yet, however one can also get an efficiency increase by simply surrounding a solar panel with a white panel.  I would assume even better with glass mirror?  Yeah, a hail storm might wreak havoc on the plate glass mirrors, however easily and cheaply replaced.

replies.author: @RWBHere
replies.text: Aluminium foil is easier to shape into an approximate parabola. You are creating a solar oven, in effect, except that you are interested in current, not the heat. It's important to avoid overheating the cells, because efficiency drops as temperatures rise.

replies.author: @stevetobias4890
replies.text: @RWBHere I was thinking more of a rectangular concave surface to act as a diflector rather than a parabolic concentrator. Similar to a torch reflector.

author: @aaknuu
text: If I remember correctly solar panels currently use a specific band gap that relates to colored light. Most solar panels only grab a specific light spectrum. Once you figure out which light spectrum your solar cell uses you could filter out the rest of the light prior to hitting the solar panel and that way you don't over heat it.

author: @wedding_photography
text:
The problem with concentrating light on solar panels is it heats them up, massively reducing the efficiency and the life span. They do have high-temperature solar panels, but those are special and expensive. 
If you want a truly cheap solution, get a space blanket - they are super cheap and super reflective.

@ThinkingandTinkering I think the question DMonZ1988 asked was a reasonable one,
If the mirror was tested first then it would give a good reference to then build upon, and we could see the extra power gained with each lense setup,

Also, I think it is rather unfair to call one of your viewers and supporters 'Lazy' because they don't have the materials to test it themselves,
I thought this channel was all about sharing ideas and cultivating a sense of "I wonder what would happen if...."

Sometimes people have the materials at hand, sometimes they don't... Should this stop them from wondering and asking?
